data.title: NetScout: Steady Progress As Expected
data.description:
The last time I wrote about NetScout Systems (NASDAQ: NTCT), my position was that this is a company with great growth prospects. Why? Well, it has solid foundations in terms of high-margin recurring revenue, operations, leadership, and cost management. Three months and another earnings report down the line, my confidence is only growing, even though the stock price has dipped a bit since then.

Full-year results for FY2025 are in, and they’re largely on the positive side. Earnings came in higher than the market expected even though its Service Assurance business had some challenges. Cybersecurity was the main driver of that growth, though, at +7% YoY for the year. But the company missed its non-GAAP EPS by $1.01. Still, I believe that the signs are there that NetScout is making good on its potential entering fiscal year 2026. Let’s talk about why.

Demand in Cybersecurity and Enterprise is Still Solid

Clearly, NetScout’s main growth vehicle is its cybersecurity enterprise solutions. After all, total revenue in FY2025 was flat, but revenue from cybersecurity grew 7% YoY, driven by more demand from its enterprise customers.

In my opinion, that growth in cybersecurity isn’t happening in a vacuum—the CAGR for the global cybersecurity market is 7.5% over the next 4 years. Cybercrimes are becoming more sophisticated and happening far more often, and it’s obvious that the main drivers are geopolitical tensions and rapid advances in automation, AI, and machine learning. In fact, “...nearly 60% of organizations state that geopolitical tensions have affected their cybersecurity strategy,” according to the World Economic Forum’s Global Cybersecurity Outlook for 2025.

So, then, the demand for solutions like NetScout’s Adaptive DDoS and Mobile Security is increasing. Everyone, from enterprises to service providers, is investing a lot in scalable, AI-augmented cybersecurity measures.

Now, if I look at the enterprise market, which NetScout services with its network performance and visibility solutions, the picture here is rosy, too. More businesses are investing in digital transformation and better network visibility. It’s not a short-term trend because more organizations want to improve their edge monitoring and 5G network operations, two key areas where NetScout is a market leader.

Moving on to specific product offerings, NetScout’s Sightline Detection, TMS Mitigation Solutions, and Smart Data from Deep Packet Inspection are incredibly valuable to its new and existing customers. And we can see this with its contracts, like the mid-seven-figure deal the company just signed with a leading cloud service provider. That deal expands an existing relationship and is supposed to improve the customer’s network and data security using NetScout’s Sightline detection and TMS mitigation solutions. I think it’s reasonable to say that deals like these show us that the company is seeing success with bigger, more integrated solutions in the cloud services and data center sectors.

The way I see it, that strong momentum in cybersecurity and the enterprise market represents the long-term value NetScout is building. The company’s solutions portfolio is pretty extensive and well-received, and it’d be fair to say it will capture a larger market share over the next few years. It makes sense that we remember that, even when accounting for slowdowns in its legacy segments like Service Assurance, where revenue dropped by around 4% in FY2025. Even with that, management is still confident that its service provider customers will “invest in 5G initiatives at a measured pace.”

Leadership Continuity is Crucial

During the company’s last earnings call on May 8, 2025, CEO Anil Singhal announced that COO Michael Szabados and CFO Jean Bua are retiring at the end of the month. At the same time, their deputies will move into those positions—Sanjay Munshi is set to step into the COO role, and Tony Piazza will take over as CFO.

Big news, yes. For one, most stakeholders didn’t exactly expect the news at this time. Still, it’s the logical move, and I don’t think that the continuity in leadership can be overemphasized, especially for a company like NetScout. I’m particularly confident that the company knows what it’s doing because the well-planned succession strategy means that it has avoided unnecessary uncertainty within the organization. I also can’t overstate how important it is for the culture when employees and the industry can see that the company invests in maintaining a strong internal leadership pipeline.

Also, the leadership transition coming now, when NetScout is making big moves in its cybersecurity and enterprise services business, demonstrates its stability. We can see that the company is well-placed to continue executing on its current strategy without any disruption because the new crop of leaders have been active in shaping its direction for years.

In my view, the smooth transition is vital to maintaining investor confidence. After all, NetScout is focused on building its cybersecurity portfolio and becoming more profitable, so having leaders who can execute its long-term goals is ideal. As an investor, knowing that there’s continuity within the company is encouraging. Why? It ensures that the company’s strategic priorities and operational efficiency are intact.

The Numbers Don’t Lie

The last thing I want to discuss is NetScout’s position in the market. Granted, the company still has a major chunk of its revenue coming from selling communications hardware, but over 80% of its revenue now comes from software sales. This is especially important when we consider the impact that tariffs can have on a business that generates 57% of its revenue in the US and the other 43% in international markets.

The positive thing, though, is that reliance on software sales means that NetScout has little to no exposure to tariff impacts and supply chain disruption. On the hardware side, it’s still safe because of USMCA exemptions for its hardware components. Of course, shifting towards software and recurring revenue hasn’t happened overnight. Still, it’s paying off now because the company can rest easy knowing that its revenue streams are more stable and predictable, regardless of the general macroeconomic environment.

Now, I want to talk about NetScout’s finances. Generally speaking, it’s in rude financial health. Free cash flow for FY2025 was $211 million; it also repaid the outstanding $75 million on its $600 million revolver. Even more importantly, this is a company that recorded revenues of $823 million and a GAAP net loss of around $368 million in FY2025, albeit that came with “total non-cash goodwill charges of $427.0 million.” By any metrics, maintaining that revenue figure compared to FY2024, which was relatively taxing, is impressive.

Having said all that, I’m fairly optimistic about its future prospects., Management’s guidance for FY2026 says that revenue will grow by ~5%, and GAAP net income should come in between $80 and $90 million, and there’s no reason to doubt that. So, on the whole, the company is heading towards profitability within the next two years. That’s not something to sleep on, given everything we know about the general state of the communications and cybersecurity industries.

Valuation

In my view, the picture hasn't changed much regarding NTCT's growth potential. Yes, the stock price has even dipped a bit since February, but my confidence comes from it being relatively undervalued in market terms. The company's non-GAAP forward P/E ratio is still around 10x, lower than that of Extreme Networks (EXTR) (13x).

Risks

Let me be clear here: there is only one major thing I want to watch out for when it comes to how NetScout can stay on course. That would be the overall slowdown in demand from the telecoms industry. However, management has already shared that they expect it and it shouldn't affect the bottom line. Demand from customers for other solutions, like 5G initiatives and digital transformation systems will still be steady, so I'm unbothered by any revenue drop from the telcos.

Tariff risks are there, on some level, especially with how randomly the current US administration keeps announcing and then backtracking on enforcement. On the whole, though, NTCT looks safe on that front, so the general mood looks to be one of cautious, justifiable optimism.

Your Takeaway

TL;DR: NetScout is moving strategically in a positive direction, and all the signs are there that it is an appealing long-term investment. Why? Well,

  • Its leadership transition is well-managed,

  • Its key drivers are in high-demand industries,

  • It is insulated from tariff impacts, and

  • Its operations are built on a solid business model

In my view, the play here is simple: the company is a good option to explore if you want to capitalize on long-term growth in cybersecurity and enterprise services. And right now, the stock appears undervalued, even more so when you see my breakdown of its potential in my previous article.

data.title: Bayer Stock Investment Could Pay Off, But It Is Not For The Faint Of Heart
data.description:
A lot has happened since my last article about Bayer A.G. (OTCPK:BAYZF, OTCPK:BAYRY). The company’s stock price is trading near multi-year lows. And the company has recently published its earnings results, which have disappointed many investors. In my view, the company’s turnaround could make the stock price surge. But the question is when it will be. The stock is very cheap, but it is not appropriate for conservative investors.

Recap of my previous article about Bayer

In my previous article, I wrote that Bayer's stock was undervalued and offered potential for long-term recovery. Bayer’s were quite strong for a while. But earnings did not show sustainable growth. The company’s key growth stimuli included resolving glyphosate and new drugs. The risks were litigation expenses and debt. I wrote that the recent set of results was only a temporary decline.

However, the decline is still ongoing, and it is not clear when it will be over. At the same time, on the bright side, we see that cash flow from operations is rising. But let me go into more details about the company’s earnings results.

Bayer’s earnings results

Bayer’s Q1 2025 profitability results came in below expectations. However, the corporation’s cash flow figures were substantially better compared to the same period a year ago.

Here are some of the key numbers:

The group sales totaled €13.74 billion, a 0.2% decline year-on-year. This was not much, but there was no growth either. The crop science division performed poorly, while the pharmaceuticals posted higher sales and earnings. The group EBITDA before special items declined by 7.4% to €4,085 million. However, the group EBITDA margin before special items totaled 29.7%.

The core earnings per share totaled EUR 2.49 (-11.7%), and the net income also declined. The free cash flow totaled minus EUR 1.5 billion, a substantial improvement compared to the same period a year ago. In Q1 2024, it totaled minus €2,626 million, mainly due to the improvement in operating cash flow.

As you can see above, I have circled the key income statement data. The profitability figures have substantially declined; the sales have remained almost unchanged, while the cash flow deficit has fortunately decreased.

Here are the key corporate events announced by the company.

In March this year, Bayer announced that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration had granted a Priority Review designation for Kerendia™, a medicine used for the treatment of adult patients suffering from heart failure with a left ventricular ejection fraction of more than 40%.

In April this year, the company also submitted an application for a third indication of Eylea™ 8 mg with the European Medicines Agency. It is likely that the medicine used to treat patients with macular edema following retinal vein occlusion will be approved, given the positive results from the global Phase III QUASAR study.

Among the efficiency measures in the crop science division was the company’s decision to reorganize the division’s activities in the areas of crop protection, production, and research and development in Germany. As part of these changes, the division will stop its operations in Frankfurt am Main after the end of 2028. Bayer aims to sell parts of this division. The rest of them will be relocated. Production at the Dormagen site will be changed to ensure its competitiveness for the future.

So, as concerns the earnings performance of the Bayer Group, sales in Germany amounted to €791 million, a rise compared to the Q1 2024 result of €729 million.

Sales at the crop science division, meanwhile, decreased year-on-year due to regulatory impacts in the U.S. and the EU, as well as volume phasing into the second quarter. Sales at the pharmaceuticals division increased, thanks to Nubeqa™, a medicine used to treat prostate cancer, and Kerendia™. The company also recorded continued sales revenue growth for Eylea™ and the radiology business, and strong performance in the contraceptives division. These positive effects were partially offset by falling sales of Xarelto™, a medicine used to reduce heart problem risks, namely, heart attack and stroke, in adults with coronary artery disease.

The company’s consumer health division also registered a rise in sales.

In the context of Bayer’s quarterly earnings history, the recent results were not a catastrophe.

Bayer’s results history (in millions of USD except per share items)

As can be seen from the graph below, the revenues since 2022 have not changed much. The operating income has decreased since 2023 but has remained at the level recorded in 2022.

Bayer’s results history (in millions of USD, except per share items)

The company’s recent news and financial fundamentals

Bayer’s stock price decreased during Wednesday's trading session as the U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Kennedy's planned "Make America Healthy Again" report is expected to single out glyphosate, Bayer’s commonly used Roundup weedkiller. The report is expected to label pesticides as a potential cause of U.S. health problems.

The crop science division, as I have mentioned above, is about to be reformed by Bayer’s management. Yet, glyphosate-based herbicides are accountable for EUR 2,647 million of the crop science division’s sales. So, a ban on glyphosate could be a real problem.

Let me go into more details about Bayer’s financial fundamentals. In the years 2023 and 2024, the company’s annual net profits totaled ($3,246.9) million and ($2,643.7) million, respectively. The net profit TTM totals ($3,519.4) million.

As concerns the company’s balance sheet, it is not in the best shape either.

The company’s net debt only totaled $38.27 billion, much higher than the company’s competitors, whose stock tickers are given in the table below. The debt-to-equity ratio, though not too high—it should not be above 200%—is much higher than most of the companies’ peers.

The current and the covered ratios also do not suggest ample amounts of liquidity, even though the company is easily able to pay back interest on its debt.

However, Bayer’s stock seems to be undervalued compared to its peers.

Apart from the profitability ratios that are impossible to generate thanks to Bayer’s lack of net profitability, the rest of the valuation figures, including the P/B, the EV/Sales, and the EV/EBITDA, suggest substantial undervaluation compared to the sector’s median.

Also, if we have a look at Bayer’s stock price history, we will see that the company’s stock is lingering near all-time lows.

So, Bayer’s stock does not look expensive.

But for Bayer’s shares not to fall further, the company needs a turnaround as soon as possible.

Conclusion

To summarize, Bayer has several divisions, namely the pharmaceuticals and the consumer health divisions, that record rising sales. The company also plans to reform its crop sciences division to decrease the losses. On the positive side, we can also say that Bayer’s sales are not falling. Moreover, during the last quarter, the cash flow deficit has decreased. At the same time, in my view, Bayer stock is not appropriate for conservative investors. The turnaround is not certain, while the already cheap Bayer A.G. stock can still keep depreciating if the company does not start reporting improving profitability figures.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Full Throttle On Deere - This Stock's A Beast
data.description:
Introduction

Guess who just made a new all-time high?

Although the title of this article spoils it, the answer is Deere & Company (NYSE:DE), the world's largest agriculture and equipment producer from Moline, Illinois.

The company, which is featured in countless country songs, made a new all-time high this week, rising roughly 25% on a year-to-date basis.

That's a mighty impressive performance, as we are dealing with a highly cyclical stock in what has become a very challenging environment.

  • Trade war fears are still a factor.
  • Inflation is still sticky.
  • Elevated rates are pressuring the affordability of big-ticket machinery.
  • Crop prices have been under pressure.
  • Geopolitical tensions are impacting agricultural trade flows.
  • General cyclical economic weakness is still a major issue.

Then there's Deere.

After going sideways since 2021 due to cyclical headwinds, the stock's breakout has pushed its 10-year total return to more than 590%, a number miles above the S&P 500's (SP500) already impressive 231% total return.

As one can imagine, I am quite happy about these developments.

While I obviously enjoy the returns on my investment, which I executed at $175 a few years ago, the main reason I'm in a good mood is that I upgraded Deere to a Strong Buy in November, despite poor guidance and a lot of headwinds that caused sentiment among analysts to sour.

I added emphasis to the quote from my takeaway back then:

While short-term headwinds, including high interest rates and weaker farm fundamentals, continue to pressure results, Deere's proactive inventory management and impressive operational efficiency position it well for the next upcycle.

Moreover, with signs of stabilization in used equipment and potential demand tailwinds from easing monetary policy and improving agricultural fundamentals, I believe Deere could be at the start of a powerful recovery.

Essentially, it all revolves around the risk/reward.

Given that the stock price had been moving sideways for years, investors had factored in a lot of bad news.

Now, the stock price is rebounding despite further bad news, which often indicates a sustainable bottom.

Hence, in this article, I'll update my thesis and explain why I believe the giant has significant potential for growth if we hit a bottom in cyclical growth expectations.

2Q25 Was Bad, But Not *That* Bad

The second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year wasn't pretty.

As we can see below, the company saw 18% lower equipment sales. Net income fell by 24% due to lower net income margins. Diluted EPS fell by 22%, as buybacks were able to offset some net income headwinds.

Taking a closer look at its Production & Precision Ag segment, which I like to call the "large agriculture" segment, as it covers the largest machines, we see 21% lower net sales and an operating profit decline of roughly $500 million.

Demand weakness is clearly visible, as volume/mix accounted for a $610 million decline, only partially offset by better pricing and lower costs.

In Small Ag and Truf, which, as you can imagine, smaller tractors, equipment, and lawn/turf machinery, saw 6% lower sales and roughly unchanged operating profit, as lower volumes were offset by better pricing, warranty tailwinds, lower production costs, and savings in selling, administrative, and general costs.

Unfortunately, the same cannot be said about Construction and Forestry, as sales fell by 23%. This dragged down operating profits from $668 million in Q2 '24 to $379 million in Q2 '25, pressured by both lower volumes and lower pricing, a toxic mix for margins, as machinery companies usually find out during economic downswings.

That's the bad news.

The good news is that Deere used its earnings call to explain that all three segments performed better than expected.

According to the company, a key driver of this performance was stronger-than-expected sales volume, especially in the North American large agriculture market. Additionally, Deere's factories were able to operate efficiently, producing higher volumes without disruption.

It also helps that Deere has spent the past few quarters adjusting production volumes to lower demand to protect margins and dealer inventories.

With regard to tariffs, the company was able to shed some light on potential costs. As of mid-May (we all know how quickly facts change), the company estimates that tariffs could increase costs by more than $500 million.

This estimate includes the recent decline in reciprocal tariffs between the United States and China.

While this is bad news, there's good news, as Deere has disclosed that roughly 80% of its U.S. complete goods sales come from products that are manufactured domestically. 75% of components are sourced within the United States.

The main risks remain in midsize tractors and roadbuilding equipment sourced from Europe and component sources from Mexico and Europe. While it is very hard to quickly mitigate these risks, it helps that just a quarter of tariffs hit its large agriculture segment, the biggest moneymaker of the company.

However, although it may be hard to mitigate tariff risks, it's not impossible, as the company has worked to certify eligible products for USMCA (U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement) and ag-use-only exemptions, especially from Mexico and the neighbors up north.

The exceptions, which weren't necessary before the trade war, successfully verified the majority of affected complete goods and components, which further reduces tariff risks of Deere and its portfolio companies.

Unfortunately, certain parts of the business are hard to de-risk. This includes a lot of construction and forestry equipment, which is located overseas. This isn't because Deere is greedy, but because it has a large foreign footprint from M&A. An M&A strategy that has been wildly successful, I should add.

For example, after the Second World War, the company bought the German Lanz company, a move that made it one of the biggest producers overseas. To this day, this move has given it a stronghold in the German/European market.

It also owns the German Wirtgen company and smaller players like Kemper, which produces equipment for forage harvesters.

While these assets are currently a tariff headwind, they remain a critical part of Deere's strategy, which is one of the reasons why it's so upbeat about its future.

Deere's Future Remains Bright

One key pillar of Deere's growth has always been its focus on the premium segment. In addition to building high-quality tractors, technology has been a way to stand out.

For more than two decades, the company has been a major player in autonomous productivity, which includes AutoTrac, Starfire, and the newer technologies like fully autonomous operations supported by SpaceX/Starlink (SPACE).

These qualities have provided pricing power, which is reflected in its industry-leading margins. Its peer, AGCO (AGCO), which also sells premium tractors in North/South America and Europe, has never been able to catch up to Deere's elevated profit margins, as we can see below. Especially during the recent downturn, Deere remained strong, maintaining double-digit operating margins.

I expect this to continue, as investments in Wirtgen are paying off. During the latest trade show in Munich, the company presented a wide range of new technologies, including roadbuilding systems, advanced engines, new guidance systems, and better coordination between machines.

According to Deere's CEO, John May, this cross-segment tech deployment aligns perfectly with what Deere calls its "smart industrial strategy," launched in 2020.

Essentially, by targeting the specific jobs that customers perform in their respective industries, Deere can provide tailored solutions that solve real-world problems.

It also allows for better capital allocation.

So far, so good.

While all of these developments are very important, they are not as important as global agriculture developments for the shorter-term development of Deere's stock price.

Hence, now comes the best news of this entire article, as the company noted that the agricultural environment is stabilizing, although profitability is still under pressure.

According to the company, while trade uncertainty continues to weigh on customer sentiment, crop prices have steadied a bit. This has occurred in light of very tight global stocks driven by higher-than-expected consumption and lower-than-expected production.

Using CBOT corn prices, we see that prices bottomed in the second half of this year. Current prices are above the pre-pandemic average.

When excluding China, global grain and oilseed stock-to-use ratios are at roughly 20-year lows, which indicates extremely tight supply and a razor-thin margin of error. The confirming data below (from Nutrien (NTR)) confirms this.

By razor-thin margins, I mean that any favorable supply or demand developments can quickly translate to a steep increase in crop prices.

Even better, Deere isn't the only one with an upbeat view, as the Purdue University Center for Commercial Agriculture with CME Group, has an indicator that shows a very steep increase in farmer sentiment.

In addition to better prices, costs are a big factor. Deere noted that while input costs are still above long-term averages, they have declined for three consecutive years. Moreover, nearly 75% of all payments under the $10 billion Emergency Commodity assistance Program have been distributed, providing a lot of (much-needed) liquidity for farmers.

Furthermore, the recent trade deal with the U.K. has brought U.S. ag commodities and bio-based energy products to the forefront of policy discussions.

This could open up incremental demand and provide price support for American producers, which benefits Deere.

Beyond its own borders, the company sees rebounds in corn and soybean profitability, higher equipment demand, and better crop yields in key markets like Brazil.

While Europe remains weak, these nations could provide a lot of demand growth in the years and decades ahead, especially as they become bigger suppliers to China.

Based on everything discussed in this article, the company widened its full-year guidance, expecting between $4.75 billion and $5.50 billion in net income. It sees no less than $4.5 billion in net operating cash flow.

This guidance includes:

  • Up to 15% lower construction/forestry and small agriculture sales.
  • Between 15% and 20% sales contraction in the large agriculture segment.
  • Roughly 10% lower industry-wide construction sales in North America.
  • A 30% decline in industry-wide large agriculture sales in North America.

So, why did investors love it so much?

Deere's Shareholder Value

In investing, it's all about the second derivative, which tells us whether the curve is concave up or concave down at that point. Or to put it in simpler terms, the rate of change matters.

While global agriculture is still in a downswing, the news that conditions are stabilizing is enough for the market to jump back into Deere.

The same goes for slower growth after a massive bull market. While the good news usually continues for a few quarters, the market is already selling into strength, expecting a slowdown in demand.

This also makes it hard to put a valuation on certain stocks during these early stages of a reversal.

Currently, America's leading agricultural equipment provider trades at a blended P/E ratio of 23.5x, a mile above its 20-year average of 14.4x. Its ten-year average is 16.1x, which doesn't help us much either.

The key to higher prices is earnings expectations.

See, six months ago, analysts expected Deere to generate $31.40 in FY2027 EPS. Now, that number is closer to $25.00.

Applying a 20x multiple to these old estimates gives us a fair stock price of roughly $630, $30 above my initial long-term target of $600.

I believe if we were to see a bottom in agricultural fundamentals, followed by upside momentum, Deere can easily generate $34-$35 in EPS by then, implying a stock price of roughly $700, 36% above the current price.

After maintaining my $600 target for roughly three years, I am upping it to $700.

While Deere is not without struggles in this environment, I remain bullish and ready to buy more on dips, as I also like its dividend growth profile.

Deere, which maintains an A-rated balance sheet, has nearly doubled its dividend since 2020. While it tends to keep a steady dividend during tough times, it always boosts its dividend in line with earnings growth during upswings.

Although a 1.3% yield isn't something to write home about for most investors, it comes with a 30% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 15.3%, which is mighty impressive and likely to last if we get a demand upswing.

It has also bought back 17% of its shares over the past ten years, which substantially improved the per-share value of the business.

All things considered, I haven't regretted buying Deere once, and I highly doubt that will be the case in the next few years.

Takeaway

Despite weak Q2 '25 results and persistent macro headwinds, Deere's breakout to new all-time highs isn't as silly as it may seem like.

In fact, I'm thrilled not just because I have been long since $175, but because the fundamentals behind the move confirm my conviction.

Deere is navigating a tough cycle with impressive efficiency, supported by proactive inventory management, pricing power, and meaningful tech advantages.

Moreover, with improving crop fundamentals, tighter global grain supplies, and growing signs of stabilization, Deere could be turning a critical corner.

While short-term pain isn't over, especially in construction and international segments, the risk/reward remains attractive.

Hence, I continue to believe that if we are near a bottom in the agriculture cycle, which is why I remain long and stick to my Strong Buy rating.

Risks To My Thesis

Key risks to my thesis include prolonged weakness in global crop prices, escalating trade tensions that could raise input costs or limit export demand, and persistent high interest rates that continue to pressure equipment affordability. These things can quickly end this rally and cause a 20-25% correction.

For what it's worth, if that were to happen, I would be a buyer.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club: Gaining Ground
data.description:
It's been 9 months since the last time I wrote about BJ's Wholesale Club (NYSE:BJ) . The stock's performance during this time has been +27% vs. 3% for the S&P 500, a performance I must admit has surprised me, although the results and future outlook may support it. In the previous article, I explained the thesis in great detail, so in this one I will focus on updating the most important KPIs and looking at the company's direction from now on, so it will be shorter than my previous article.

Thesis Recap

"As I will show later, it is possible that with the slowdown in inflation rates, the food segment may grow less, which could have a positive impact on margins (as long as the margins in this segment continue to grow, as they have so far).

There are two possibilities with this new incremental margin: it could be returned to customers in the form of better prices, thereby increasing the value proposition of the membership (which I believe they will choose), or it could be distributed to shareholders through buybacks or dividends. With an improvement in margins, there might be an improvement in returns and a possible re-rating to 19X-20X PE, though I will not include this scenario in my valuation.

The obsession with this type of business model lies in extreme cost savings, which can then be passed on to customers in the form of lower prices. This makes the subscription highly attractive to both potential and existing customers. The subscription model typically has margins close to 100% and represents around 2% of sales, but more than 40% of EBITDA." - From my previous article.

Important KPIs Update

The first thing I will do is update the most important KPIs, as they will help us better understand the business and its evolution over time.

Memberships: The first thing to highlight is the strong performance in memberships. As I mentioned in the previous article, I expected this business line—with EBITDA margins close to 100%—to be the fastest growing, at rates of 8% or higher. Growth for fiscal year 2025 (as BJ reports it) was 8.6% in sales for this segment. This, combined with a 4.2% increase in members—now surpassing 7.5 million—results in the same growth in revenue per member.

This will be the main growth driver—not so much in terms of total sales, since memberships represent only 2.2% of them—but in EBITDA, where they account for 43.8% compared to 40.4% last year. This is thanks to their 100% margins, versus the 3.1% margin from product sales.

"There are two possibilities with this new incremental margin: it could be returned to customers in the form of better prices, thereby increasing the value proposition of the membership (which I believe they will choose), or it could be distributed to shareholders through buybacks or dividends."

The membership renewal rate remains an impressive 90%. Additionally, a membership price increase was implemented starting January 1st. The impact this quarter has been practically negligible, but they estimate the increase will contribute around $20 million in additional revenue over the fiscal year. The good thing about price increases is that they go directly to the company’s EBITDA, as there are no direct marginal costs associated with them.

Clubs: The number of clubs is also an important metric. This year, they opened 9 new locations, bringing the total to 252. They also increased the average number of members per club, from 30,638 to 30,864. BJ is becoming increasingly attractive to consumers, positioning itself in densely populated areas and leveraging this scale to negotiate better costs with suppliers. These savings are passed on to customers in the form of lower prices, which in turn attracts new customers—spinning the flywheel once again.

CAPEX: The company’s goal is to open between 25 and 30 new clubs over the next two years, including expansion into a new state: Texas. To achieve this, it will need to increase its CAPEX. This year, they spent $588 million, and they expect to spend $800 million next year, as they plan to open a new distribution center—their fourth.

It was mentioned that the new center will efficiently support the recent expansion in Tennessee, Alabama, and Kentucky, and will align with plans to enter Texas (Dallas–Fort Worth) in 2026. The project will cost approximately $200 million, to be distributed over the next two fiscal years.

The best part is that this expansive CAPEX plan is being funded entirely with the company’s own generated cash. In fact, there was even enough left over to reduce net debt to 0.5x EBITDA and to repurchase 1.1% of the outstanding shares.

In any case, since net income has only increased by 2.1%, while all of this has significantly increased the capital employed, ROCE has declined slightly this year—and I expect it to continue this trend for at least the next two years.

One thing I found strange, and which is directly related to what I mentioned at the beginning of the article, is how, if memberships (which have significantly higher margins than product sales) have grown three times faster than product sales, the company's EBITDA and operating margins have actually decreased. The answer to this lies in the conference call.

The reason was the increase in costs of key products such as eggs and fresh items, which directly impact the consumer. BJ’s chose not to fully pass these cost increases on to customers, prioritizing the preservation of its value proposition.

This aligns perfectly with what I previously stated: although BJ’s may be able to achieve better margins over time, I don't expect this to be reflected in the income statement. Instead, those gains will be returned to consumers in the form of lower prices. In this way, margins don’t expand, but the company delivers significantly more value to customers, grows through higher volumes, and once again spins the shared economies of scale flywheel.

"That was really due to taking care of our members in the face of some rising commodities. And like you've heard say before, we will always invest in price." Robert Eddy, CEO.

Other data points: private label now represents 26% of total sales (with a target of 30%). Digital sales grew 26% year-over-year in Q4, and are up 53% over the past two years. 60% of members interact digitally, and 90% of the digital channel is fulfilled through physical stores.

Fresh 2.0:It has increased both traffic and total sales, and the company aims to expand it to more fresh categories.

Risks And Valuation

BJ is a very defensive company, so it doesn't present any new risks beyond those mentioned in my previous article. The only update worth noting is the company's comment regarding tariffs and duties:

Given the evolving landscape, we are not contemplating the impact of tariffs on our current assumptions. Tariffs could shape the trajectory of inflation and broader consumer demand and ultimately influence our results for the year.

We all participate and live in a consumer-led economy and tariffs, while potentially useful in some regards are likely to raise prices for Americans. In fact, cost of key commodities are already moving up. Tariffs also risks some supply chain disruption as the market moves production around to mitigate tariff exposures.

With that said, periods of rising prices and supply chain disruption have often been good for our company. When consumer wallets are stretched, most consumers search for value, they come to our channel and they come to BJ's. We also have lesser exposure to tariffs than many retailers out there. And to your -- the point of your question, we have robust muscle around dealing with inflation in our COGS base.

Certainly, the exposure to Canada and Mexico is greater given the variety of products that they bring in and it does touch our grocery business for sure. We don't believe we're differentially affected negatively by any exposure in Canada or Mexico at this point. And hopefully, we're able to figure our way through those tariffs as well. We'll use that same muscle that I talked about in negotiating with our suppliers moving things around, doing all the things that we know how to do to buy things at the lowest cost so we can give that value to our members." Robert Eddy, CEO.

For the valuation, I have done the following: I believe that the sales of products and merchandise will normalize to around 3% growth, but that the EBITDA margins for this segment will improve slightly. On the other hand, I think the membership segment will continue to grow at 9%, and I am not factoring in the likely $5 increase in membership fees, which would go directly to the company's net profit.

With this, BJ's EBITDA margins should expand to 6.2%. If they effectively improve working capital management, the conversion of EBITDA to net income and free cash flow should also improve. But as I’ve already mentioned, I don’t expect the margin to actually reach that figure; it’s more of a representative benchmark of potential margin. The real margin will likely remain similar to the current level, but the company will have achieved better prices for the end customer, attracting new members and enabling the opening of new clubs across the country.

In this way, we arrive at the EPS that the company can generate over the next five years. If we apply a P/E multiple of 20X, although I also have a scenario with a P/E multiple of 18X, the target prices in five years range between $115 and $128. As of the date of this writing, BJ's is trading at $108, which would yield very poor returns of 1.3-3.4%.

Since BJ is a defensive and well-managed company, and given that it offers such low expected IRRs, I can't give it a sell rating. That's why I rate the stock as a hold and would wait for better prices that at least offer me a 10%-12% return over 5 years.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Stock: Seems Like A No-Brainer At These Levels
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to start coverage of Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) as it fell from its highs on the back of the success of its obesity drugs, only to find that it is performing as well as it has in the past if not better. I have come to conclusion that the company’s share price was driven primarily by the negative sentiment, which presents a good opportunity to start a position, or accumulate further.

Briefly on Performance

Over the last year, the company’s shares have lost half of their value after experiencing a significant run-up driven by the success of the miracle drugs Ozempic and Wegovy. Since then, the company has faced significant competition from Eli Lilly’s (LLY) drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound, which have outperformed in clinical studies, demonstrating greater weight loss in patients. In Early 2025, U.S. prescription data showed that LLY’s Zepbound drug surpassed Wegovy, another hit to NVO’s market share. Further eroding NVO’s market share was a period of supply shortages for Wegovy and Ozempic, during which U.S. compounding industries began to produce cheaper, unbranded versions of semaglutide. To add insult to injury, the company’s next major obesity drug candidate, CagriSema, failed to deliver the promised jump in efficacy over LLY’s Zepbound in late-stage clinical trials, further dampening share price sentiment.

Over the same time as the company’s shares began their descent, NVO’s sales continued to climb. And at a decent pace, too! In the last 5 quarters, the company averaged around 23% y/y growth. For such a large company, that is an impressive y/y growth.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability, margins across the board are fantastic. The company is highly profitable, with margins staying relatively stable over the last year. If we zoom out a little to look at the trend of profitability, we can see there has been a decent boost over time. Clearly, we can see the runway success of the GLP-1 drugs helped the company’s already profitable business even more.

Continuing on with efficiency and profitability, the company’s ROA and ROE are still very impressive, even after the downtrend it has been experiencing recently. NVO’s ROE especially stands out not just in the pharmaceutical sector but across the broader market. As the company’s net margins improved, so did its ROA and ROE, and being an asset-light, cash-generating business, NVO doesn’t require heavy capital investments relative to its earnings, allowing the company to generate substantial profits without the need to reinvest large sums back into physical assets and working capital. By consistently returning capital to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks, the company maintains a relatively low equity base, leading to a sharp rise in ROE. ROA is also quite impressive, which means the management is very capable of utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital efficiently.

The next important metric I like to look at is the company’s ROTC and how it compares to its peers. Here, I gathered a few companies that already have a pipeline of similar obesity and diabetes drugs as well as some that will be in production and are in the pipeline, like:

  • Pfizer (PFE), which expects to have its drug launched in 2028, as well as
  • Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and
  • Amgen (AMGN), although a lot later (2030, according to Internet search).

Here we can see NVO’s ROTC is exceptionally high and is leading the pack by a long mile. This tells us the company’s profitability and capital discipline are hard to match, and it is in a unique position in the market when it comes to its GLP-1 drug portfolio. With all the competition, you would think there would be some erosion in its moat, however, it still is very strong, and its competitive advantage is undeniable. For a company with such a high ROTC, I would be willing to pay a premium.

What about the company’s financial position? The company ended the latest quarter with around $6B in cash and equivalents, against around $14B in long-term debt. With such high margins, I would be very surprised if this amount of debt would be crippling. Although it is paying quite a bit in interest expenses, however, it is also bringing in a lot from interest income, bringing its net interest expense considerably lower, and its interest coverage ratio was around 22x if we are not being conservative. If we want to be a bit more conservative, the company’s interest cover ratio without interest income was 7.5x, which is still well over what analysts consider to be healthy (2x) and even better than my conservative requirements of at least 5x, therefore, I don’t see any liquidity or insolvency issues here.

Overall, all metrics look outstanding across the board. Sure, there may have been some slight downtrends in some metrics, like ROA and ROE, however, these are still very attractive returns. The company’s revenues are still growing at an amazing pace; margins are steady and improving. The company is highly profitable with plenty of resources for further growth.

Comments on the Outlook

So, what’s happening? It seems that investors are very negative on the company due to the competition and the perceived loss of market share, driving the company’s share way down in just one year. The most recent negative news that came out just this morning was that the CEO is going to step down because of the recent share performance.

In my view, if the share price is falling on this news, it seems that investors did not take this as a positive outcome. Lars Fruergaard’s tenure as a CEO has been marked by a Transformation into a global powerhouse, evolving the company from a diabetes-focused to a global leader in obesity and metabolic disease, with the help of Wegovy and Ozempic. The success of the GLP-1 drugs not only transformed NVO but also set new standards for obesity and diabetes treatment globally. The recent missteps of losing market share in obesity drugs, an underwhelming pipeline, and the mishandling of CagriSema, which was touted as the next big leap forward, were enough to force the CEO to step down. The communication regarding the flexible dosing protocol, which only about 62% of participants reached the highest dose by week 68, was highly criticized by investors and analysts alike. There was also some confusion regarding the lack of immediate disclosure of key secondary endpoints, like blood sugar reductions.

So, the next big product’s failure and the lack of proper communication were enough to push him out. It’s understandable. The company has lost its next product for now under the CEO. Time to move on.

The company also cut its guidance, as Wegovy sales plunged 13% q/q, which missed analysts’ consensus. The company’s peers that sell the GLP-1 drug are gaining market share against NVO, especially LLY’s Zepbound, which contributed to the company’s cut sales outlook, as well as operating profit. Nevertheless, these are still very good numbers for the year.

The current tariff situation will have some impact on the company’s performance as well; however, to combat this, the company has heavily invested in U.S. manufacturing, including a $2.5B API production facility and a $4B expansion in North Carolina, which is meant to double its footprint in the U.S. By producing active pharmaceutical ingredients domestically, in the U.S. or Denmark, NVO can reduce tariff-related costs, ensuring a secure supply chain. The now former CEO recently said that he “doesn’t have significant concerns regarding the direct impact of tariffs” on the company.

The company is still highly profitable. I’m sure in the future whoever the new CEO is will lead the company forward and make sure that NVO doesn’t drop the ball and get back on track in developing new obesity drugs to maintain its profitability.

Valuation

Let’s look at a quick valuation model to see what the company looks like over the next few years. Starting with revenues. Analysts expect the company to still grow at quite a decent pace over the next few years. After a few years, it is hard to come up with decent estimates, therefore, I won’t use analysts’ figures and taper the sales down, bringing the company’s CAGR over the next decade to around 12% CAGR, which is much slower than its 5-year CAGR of 18.5% and its 3-year CAGR of 27%.

For EPS and margins, I went quite conservative and only increased the company’s EBIT margins by 200bps over the next decade. The company managed to improve its EBIT margins by 200bps from FY22 to FY23, so another 200bps over a decade is achievable in my opinion.

For my DCF model, I decided not to use the company’s WACC of around 5.4% because it is slightly too low. It is so low because its beta right now is 0.21. To be a bit more conservative, I am adding another 150bps to its WACC, bringing it closer to 7%.

Additionally, I am using a terminal growth rate of 2.5% and discounting the final intrinsic value by 15% to be even more conservative. With that said, NVO’s intrinsic value is around $126 a share, which means it is trading at quite a discount right now because of the negative sentiment surrounding the company.

Closing Comments

The current negativity surrounding Novo Nordisk A/S presents an opportunity, and the decrease in the company’s value by over half should be seen as a blessing for those who missed out on the previous run-up, like me. The company is still very profitable, with fantastic profitability and efficiency metrics and double-digit growth.

There are some risks here if the company can’t get out of that negative sentiment, but most companies go through such phases and eventually will become a darling on the Street. I will be looking out for further developments on future obesity drugs, as well as monitoring the progress of the change in management.

Even with quite conservative assumptions in my model, the company seems to be a steal at these prices, therefore, I am initiating my coverage of Novo Nordisk A/S with a Buy rating. I also started a small position because this seems like a good opportunity to see where this is going over the next few years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD's AI Strategy Sprints Past Intel, Firmly In The Fast Lane
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Azlaan Mansuri as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) has been able to introduce more robust products and increase its market flexibility thanks to its partnership with TSMC and its chiplet manufacturing strategy. Due to a number of difficulties Intel has faced recently, AMD has increased its market share in the PC CPU industry. AMD's lucrative P/E and PEG ratios place it in a strategic position for potential growth, making it a cheaper option compared to its peers.

On 14th May 2025, AMD's board of directors approved a share buyback plan worth $6 billion, propelling the total authorized repurchase program to $10 billion, a move that could attract value investors.

The Competitive Scene

Domestic Market

AMD and Intel (INTC) compete heavily in their AI interface strategy. Intel's Gaudi AI challenges AMD's competitive stance in the AI accelerator market, especially with the new Gaudi 3 model launched by Intel's Habana Labs. The central competitive landscape in the industry centers on AI training and inference tasks, where Gaudi 3 directly competes against AMD's Instinct MI300 series. Intel claims that Gaudi 3 is equipped with advantages here, such as high throughput, low latency, and scalability that support the use of multiple data types. Apart from this, the Gaudi 3's connection protocol is Ethernet, which is more cost-effective for customers as the connection option scales with lower expenses than AMD’s Infinity Fabric (which is proprietary).

Meanwhile, AMD's MI300X is equipped with 192GB of HBM3 memory, which is triple the amount of HBM2 memory compared to previous models. It also has better bandwidth, important for utilization in high-performance computing (HPC) and AI-related tasks. Along with a well-elaborated ROCm software platform, AMD also projects success in the AI chips sector, as it expects $4.5 billion in revenues from that segment. Hence, AMD sits comfortably in its strong position in the market with its established presence over Gaudi 3. Gaudi 3 again promises savings, but compared to the raw power for training bigger AI models by AMD and Nvidia, it is less focused. Its software ecosystem is expanding; however, it is still behind AMD's ROCm and Nvidia's CUDA.io. Partnerships between Intel and OEMs like Dell and HPE facilitate entry to the enterprise market, so Gaudi 3 still poses a threat to AMD in cost-sensitive sectors. At the same time, AMD's superior hardware and ecosystem bolster its advantage. AMD has to be attentive to Intel's endeavors as Gaudi 3 integrates into the enterprise space. The consequence of that may be seen in more innovation, which in turn may lead to a refreshed AI accelerator arena.

Chinese Competition

Biren

Biren Technology, a new, privately held semiconductor company located in China, is pursuing a lead over AMD in the GPU market, especially in artificial intelligence (AI) and high-performance computing (HPC). Its chips can be used in different models, such as the BR100 and BR104, specifically designed for the demanding tasks of artificial intelligence and high-performance computing. The BR100, which has 77 billion transistors and a 7nm manufacturing process, is a strong competitor to AMD's capabilities.

However, Biren has experienced a setback due to the U.S. export control laws, as TSMC, a crucial chip-maker and that produces its chips, suspended production of its chips. This forces Biren to seek other foundries, probably leading to stunted scalability and global outreach. The company is also creating software as a stack for its chips, besides developing the chips themselves, which is a very important step for entering the AI and HPC markets. In this case, AMD has an advantage with its well-established Radeon and Instinct GPU brands and its strong ROCm software ecosystem, showing its clear edge in product maturity and adoption. On the other hand, the products from Biren's GPUs are—for the most part—more effective in standalone AI tasks. Nonetheless, software problems and production issues limit Biren from competing with AMD on a global scale. Diversified product lines and well-founded infrastructure are reasons why AMD overcomes this new player.

Huawei

Huawei, through its HiSilicon subsidiary, competes with AMD mainly in extremely integrated AI chips and processors. Due to the disparity in their manufacturing capabilities and ecosystem development, AMD has a technological edge. AMD has a clear upper hand due to its partnership with TSMC by using the more advanced 5nm and the cutting-edge 3nm processes for its CPUs and GPUs. These superior technologies significantly enhance performance, energy efficiency and ensure more transistor doping without additional cost. In contrast, Huawei, which has been hampered by U.S. sanctions, is using SMIC's older 7nm and 14nm processes. Lacking access to extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography, Huawei's chips have had a rough time keeping up with the efficiency and power of AMD, explaining the capabilities of the gap. The Ascend 910B, which is aimed at AI workloads in data centers, has not yet been proven to be as robust and effective as AMD's Instinct MI300 series, which provides an advanced design and optimized software.

Growth Drivers

Hardware Innovations

Zen 4 Architecture: Precision Engineering for Performance

AMD's Zen 4 architecture, powering the Ryzen 7000 series for consumers and EPYC 9004 series for servers, is a major leap in CPU design and introduces a new era of CPU architectural innovation. The wide dispatch pipelines and larger caches responsible for the 13% increase in instructions per clock (IPC) from architectural enhancements result in the possibility of a more efficient task execution. Therefore, users enjoy significant improvements. For example, in gaming, Cyberpunk 2077 now plays at a higher frame rate, and generates 20% more profit compared to Zen 3, while video editor Premiere Pro has a process completion of 15-25% ​​faster due to this new architecture.

The new DDR5 memory control facilitates increased memory bandwidth by 100%, compared to the former DDR4, which eventually goes up to 5200 MT/s in main consumer configurations. This is great for applications like 3D rendering or large-scale simulations, which suffer from data bottlenecks that stall performance. The move to a 5nm process that Zen 4 adopts (by TSMC) increases the power efficiency and allows the company to get 96 cores inside a single EPYC processor. In this case, the high percentage of the core count means fewer servers are needed, which equals less hardware and energy consumption costs, thus making the EPYC a top choice for hyperscalers like Google Cloud.

RDNA 3: GPU Powerhouse for Gaming and Beyond

The RDNA 3 architecture present in Radeon RX 7000 GPUs and Instinct MI300 accelerators demonstrates a 50% performance-per-watt uplift over RDNA 2, all thanks to a chiplet-based design and a refined 5nm process. The Infinity Cache acts as a low-latency buffer, cutting the VRAM dependency off traditional and boosting 4K gameplay performance by 30-40% in benchmarks such as Flight Simulator 2020. Scalable Ray-Tracing Cores On Par With NVIDIA RTX 4000 Series Schematic Lifelike Reflections in games Like Resident Evil Village with Minor Frame Rate Drops, balancing performance and quality visuals.

The RDNA 3 goes beyond just gaming and shines in AI and HPC as well. The Instinct MI300 integrated with the CPU-GPU architecture leads to twice the acceleration of AI training workloads, such as those in natural language processing, compared to discrete GPU setups. This combined design reduces the data transfer overhead that is a common bottleneck in AI compute, and it puts AMD in a position to challenge NVIDIA’s A100 and H100 for supremacy in the data centers.

Software Ecosystem: Unlocking Hardware Potential

ROCm: Open-Source Innovation for AI and Science

The open-source ROCm platform, AMD's solution for GPU-accelerated computing, works in stark contrast to the closed NVIDIA CUDA ecosystem and explains the flexibility for broader adoption. An example of this in AI is seen with researchers at CERN who used ROCm to run realistic simulations using Radeon Instinct GPUs, achieving a 1.5x acceleration in particle collision data processing compared to CPU-only setups. Meta is another company that has used ROCm to train recommendation models, using its flexibility to optimize performance across AMD's GPU lineup. Its developer-friendly orientation ensures the continuous relevancy of AMD's hardware in the course of changes in the development of AI and HPC workloads.

Cloud and Enterprise: EPYC’s Data Center Dominance

In the enterprise business area, the AMD EPYC processors power cloud operators like AWS and Azure. AWS has EC2 instances based on EPYC that are more cost-effective than the Intel-based instances, with better price-performance by 15% on workloads such as database hosting. The use of EPYC in Google's Tau VMs, which also covers machine learning inference at scale, is a testament to AMD's enterprise-grade reliability. The collaboration not only validates AMD of the enterprise level's strong reliability but also fuels its server market share up to 18.6% in Q2 2023 per IDC.

DCF Model

The fair value of AMD’s share price is $151.46, presenting an upside of 28.7% at the time of this writing. The model’s projections are in line with AMD's median performances over the past 5 years. This approach does not account for recent products and services’ technological dominance, along with Intel’s secular decline in the PC-CPU market. If taken into account, this would translate into even higher growth potential.

Valuation Multiples

For the last twelve months (TTM) and the forward years (FY1–FY3), AMD’s Non-GAAP P/E ratios that focus solely on core operating earnings are consistently below the industry average. In comparison with others like (NVDA) (FY1: 27.97, TTM: 41.11) and (TXN) (FY1: 33.74), AMD is anticipated to be undervalued as per its expected earnings. This implies that the market doesn't fully factor in its considerable growth potential. The lower Non-GAAP P/E ratios speak volumes that AMD's stock is relatively cheap considering its future profit trajectory—a strong point to emphasize to investors. The higher GAAP P/E forward ratio spells caution, which is understandable from the standpoint of higher perceived risks or costs that are not included in Non-GAAP figures. Yet, the below-average TTM GAAP P/E mitigates this concern. A PEG that is under the magical figure of 1.0 (especially TTM at 0.79) makes it abundantly clear that the genuine growth of AMD has not been taken entirely into account in the valuation, making it appealing as an investment choice vis-a-vis the higher PEGs of rivals. Although slightly more than Nvidia's, AMD is characterized as a value-growth play in the face of Nvidia's unwavering stance.

Key Risks

Internal Development Clusters

Despite improvements over the last four months, AMD's internal development clusters still lack the resources needed to effectively compete in the long-term GPU development landscape. As of right now, AMD states that it has rented 8,000 MI300 GPUs in total from CSPs spread across multiple clusters, with the largest cluster housing roughly 2,000 MI300 GPUs. A more thorough analysis, however, indicates that the company's actual consistent availability might be closer to 3,000–4,000 GPUs. Larger-scale projects and cooperative development initiatives are impacted by this restriction, and a significant expansion in GPU availability is required. AMD's limited cluster-level resources for individual internal developers also make it more difficult for the company to compete and innovate in the rapidly changing GPU development market. However, AMD has $6.05 billion in cash and cash equivalents, giving it the financial flexibility to take a longer-term, more strategic approach to investing. AMD should take advantage of this edge and make long-term, multi-year commitments instead of continuing with its current sub-year cluster strategy. This includes investing in a sizable cluster of more than 10,000 flagship-class GPUs. This would show AMD's commitment to each one.

Underwhelming Developer Engagement

NVIDIA's annual GTC developer conference features more than 500 developer-focused sessions and interactive labs spanning different AI stack layers. AMD, however, does not have a GTC-style conference with many sessions geared toward developers. The multi-track developer sessions that are provided at GTC are absent from the company's June "Advancing AI" event, which instead concentrates on product keynotes and prerecorded talks. AMD should introduce a yearly, in-person ROCm Developer Conference with parallel tracks covering kernel authoring, graph compilers, HIP/Triton migration, MI300 cluster tuning, and real-time debugging with the ROCm toolchain to address this issue. This would give ROCm users a forum to discuss war stories, report blocking issues, and create the community that made GTC so important to the CUDA community.

Recommendation

AMD’s sustenance depends on the state-of-the-art advancement of hardware, specifically the Zen 4 and RDNA 3 architectures, along with an ecosystem of software that wholly maximizes their reach. In the gaming and cloud strategic vertical expansion, the company utilizes recent interconnections, showing a broad mindset. Meanwhile, a focus on AI, HPC, and edge computing indicates it's long-term plans. Nevertheless, even with tough competition on the horizon, AMD’s competitive edge in terms of technology, flexibility, and reacting to industry needs shows its clout and capability, and its likelihood to continue to be a leader in computing going forward.

AMD trades at a lower ratio than its competitors in most of the multiples. Based on the forward PEG ratios, high teens to 30%+ EPS growth over the next two to three years can be projected. Simultaneously, AMD's promising data-center and AI sectors with both osmosis in EPS forecasts and a lower valuation than peers indicate that there appears to be a value realization as investors bring shares in alignment with fair value. Given my valuation of ~$151 per share, which doesn't factor in the current advancements discussed here, I see at least a 27%-30% upside.
